personality_voice.book_baseline: Lee Jordan is defined by irreverent, partisan Quidditch commentary that repeatedly draws McGonagall's rebukes - he calls Slytherin fouls 'disgusting cheating,' shouts 'YOU FILTHY, CHEATING B--,' swears so badly McGonagall tries to grab the megaphone, and openly admits bias ('I'm telling it like it is, Professor!'). Beyond commentary, he is enthusiastic, socially gregarious, and mischievous - he feeds Skiving Snackboxes to first-years, puts Nifflers in Umbridge's office, exploits Educational Decree loopholes to needle Umbridge, and joins Fred and George in most of their schemes. In Potterwatch he is confident, witty, and politically courageous - hosting an illegal resistance broadcast under the name 'River,' making dry jokes about Death Eaters, and maintaining composure while reporting murders. His voice is consistently energetic, direct, and laced with humor even under pressure.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus contains only one Quidditch match (PS/SS) where Lee's commentary is present. His lines are functional and neutral: 'Hello! And welcome to Hogwarts' first Quidditch game of the season!', 'Angelina Johnson scores! 10 points for Gryffindor!', 'He's got the Snitch! Harry Potter receives 150 points for catching the Snitch!' There is no bias, no McGonagall confrontation, no insults toward Slytherin, no swearing, no jokes. The commentary is indistinguishable from a neutral announcer. None of his off-pitch personality - the mischief, the Potterwatch courage, the Umbridge trolling - appears in the film corpus at all.
personality_voice.difference: The film strips out the defining feature of Lee's book voice: his partisan, boundary-pushing, McGonagall-baiting commentary style. The repeated pattern of provocative commentary followed by McGonagall's rebukes and Lee's half-apologies is entirely absent. His off-pitch personality (mischief, political courage, wit) has no film representation in the corpus.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The book establishes Lee's personality primarily through his commentary voice and his mischievous off-pitch behavior. The film reduces him to a neutral announcer with no distinguishing traits. This is a significant loss of the character's defining voice. The score falls in the 14-17 range: the character remains recognizable as 'the Quidditch commentator' but the important traits - bias, irreverence, confrontational humor, political courage - are reduced or absent.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for books (multiple commentary scenes with McGonagall confrontations, Potterwatch scenes, Niffler/Umbridge scenes). Directly evidenced for films (neutral commentary lines only).

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Lee serves multiple narrative functions: (1) Quidditch commentator who provides match information while also generating comedy and tension through his bias and McGonagall's reactions; (2) active participant in Fred and George's schemes - he assists Skiving Snackbox demonstrations, puts Nifflers in Umbridge's office, exploits decree loopholes; (3) host and anchor of Potterwatch, an illegal resistance broadcast - this is a significant act of agency where he organizes, leads, and sustains a politically dangerous operation; (4) fighter in the Battle of Hogwarts, where he and George slam Yaxley to the floor.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the film corpus, Lee's only function is neutral Quidditch announcer. He announces scores and game events. There is no agency beyond narrating what other characters do. His Potterwatch role, his participation in Fred and George's schemes, his resistance activities, and his Battle of Hogwarts role are absent from the film corpus.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Potterwatch role is the most significant loss - in the books Lee is the organizer and host of an illegal resistance broadcast, which is an act of independent agency and courage. This is entirely absent from the film corpus. His role as Fred and George's active collaborator (not just bystander) is also lost. His combat role in the Battle of Hogwarts is absent.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The film reduces Lee to a single function (announcer) and removes his most significant act of agency (Potterwatch). The transfer rule applies: Potterwatch exists in the films (DH Part 1) but Lee's role as its host and organizer is not represented in the film corpus provided. His role is clearly altered - from active participant and resistance organizer to passive narrator. Score falls in the 10-13 range.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for books. Film corpus only covers PS/SS match; absence of Potterwatch and other roles is directly evidenced by the corpus provided.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Lee's motivations are not deeply explored internally - he is not a POV character. Observable motivations include: loyalty to Gryffindor and the Weasley twins (drives his partisan commentary and collaboration in schemes), opposition to Umbridge and the Death Eater regime (drives Potterwatch and the Niffler incidents), and a general enthusiasm for mischief and rule-bending. His Potterwatch work implies genuine political conviction and courage - he risks serious danger to broadcast resistance information.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film corpus gives no evidence of any motivation beyond performing commentary. There is no basis to evaluate his loyalty, his anti-authoritarian streak, or his political convictions from the film corpus alone.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film corpus does not establish or contradict his motivations - it simply provides too little material. His political conviction (Potterwatch) and loyalty-driven mischief are absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Because Lee's motivations are not deeply articulated even in the books (he is a supporting character), the absence of motivation-revealing scenes in the film corpus is partially a function of limited screen time. However, the complete absence of any motivation-revealing material - particularly the Potterwatch scenes which are the clearest expression of his convictions - means the film portrayal is too thin to support the same characterization. Score in the 8-10 range: motivations are partially flattened by absence rather than actively changed.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Reasonably inferred from books. Not enough evidence in film corpus to evaluate directly.

character_arc.book_baseline: Lee Jordan has a relatively flat arc across most of the series - he is consistently the commentator, the Weasley twins' friend, the mischief-maker. The most significant arc development is in Deathly Hallows, where he transitions from school-based mischief-maker to active resistance organizer running Potterwatch under genuine personal risk. This represents a meaningful maturation: the same irreverent energy channeled into something politically serious and dangerous.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film corpus covers only the PS/SS Quidditch match. No arc development is visible. The Deathly Hallows transition is absent from the corpus.
character_arc.difference: The DH arc - the most significant development Lee undergoes - is absent from the film corpus. However, Lee's arc is largely flat through books 1-6, so the PS/SS film material is not misrepresenting an arc that hadn't yet developed at that point.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The film corpus is limited to early material where Lee's arc is indeed flat, so the flat portrayal in the corpus is not itself a distortion. However, the DH arc (Potterwatch) represents a meaningful character development that is absent from the film corpus entirely. Since the corpus does not include DH film material, this is a corpus limitation rather than a confirmed film distortion - but the absence still means the arc's culmination cannot be evaluated as preserved. Score in the 11-13 range: arc is compressed/incomplete in the corpus but not actively contradicted.
character_arc.evidence_status: Reasonably inferred. Film corpus too limited to evaluate DH arc.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Lee's primary relationship is with Fred and George Weasley - they are described repeatedly as 'the Weasley twins' friend Lee Jordan,' and he participates in their schemes, assists their Skiving Snackbox demonstrations, joins their Triwizard Tournament age-line attempt, and is dispirited when they leave Hogwarts. This is a genuine friendship with shared values (mischief, anti-authoritarianism) and mutual loyalty. His relationship with McGonagall is a recurring comic dynamic: she monitors his commentary, threatens him, and he half-apologizes while continuing. His relationship with the broader Gryffindor community (DA membership, Potterwatch contributors) is also meaningful.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows Lee only as commentator. No relationship with Fred and George is established. The McGonagall commentary dynamic - the defining relational comedy of his book appearances - is entirely absent. He is not shown interacting with any character directly.
key_relationships.difference: The Fred-and-George friendship, which is the emotional core of Lee's book presence, is absent. The McGonagall dynamic, which generates most of his book comedy and defines his voice, is absent. These are not minor omissions - they are the relationships through which Lee is primarily characterized.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Both key relationships are absent from the film corpus. The McGonagall dynamic is particularly significant because it is present in every Quidditch match in the books and is the primary vehicle for Lee's personality. Score in the 5-6 range: important relationship dynamics are altered or weakened by complete absence.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for books. Directly evidenced as absent in film corpus.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Lee's complexity comes from several layers: (1) the tension between his role as 'official' commentator and his inability to suppress his partisanship - he is simultaneously performing a neutral function and failing at it, which is the source of his comedy; (2) his active participation in resistance against authority (Umbridge, Death Eaters) showing genuine courage beneath the mischief; (3) his loyalty to Fred and George expressed through shared risk-taking; (4) the Potterwatch scenes where his irreverent humor coexists with reporting murders and sustaining hope under dangerous conditions - this is a genuinely complex tonal register.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film corpus presents Lee as a one-dimensional neutral announcer. None of the contradictions, the partisan humor, the courage, the loyalty, or the tonal complexity of Potterwatch are present.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses: the commentary-bias-McGonagall dynamic, the Potterwatch role and its tonal complexity, the Fred-and-George collaboration, the Umbridge resistance (Nifflers, decree exploitation), the Battle of Hogwarts participation, and the dispirited reaction to Fred and George's departure. These are not peripheral details - they constitute most of what makes Lee a textured character rather than a plot function.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The film reduces Lee to a single trait (announcer) and removes the contradictions, relationships, and acts of agency that give him texture. This is a major flattening. Score in the 5-7 range.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for books. Directly evidenced as absent in film corpus.

McGonagall trying to physically take the megaphone from Lee

score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if core book function transferred or removed): Potterwatch role - Lee's most significant act of agency - is absent from film corpus. Cap applied.
Cap 1 (score above 85 if major complexity removed): Partisan commentary dynamic, Potterwatch complexity, and Fred-George relationship all absent. Cap applied but score already below 85.
comparative.key_observations: The film corpus for Lee Jordan is extremely limited - only a single PS/SS Quidditch match - and reduces him to a neutral, functional announcer with no distinguishing personality. The two defining features of his book characterization are both absent: his partisan, McGonagall-baiting commentary voice, and his Potterwatch role as resistance broadcaster. The Fred-and-George friendship, which is the relational core of his book presence, is also entirely absent. What remains in the film is recognizable as 'the Quidditch commentator' but lacks the irreverence, bias, humor, courage, and relational texture that define the character in the books. The score of 61 reflects a character who is superficially present but substantially hollowed out, with confidence held at Medium due to the limited film corpus provided.
